stories.headline: Denver Jury Convicts Man of Murder in 2022 Double Stabbing
stories.summary:
Matthew Omara was convicted Tuesday by a Denver jury on two counts of first-degree murder after deliberation and one count of second-degree assault for a 2022 attack that killed two women and injured a third. The stabbings occurred just before 8 a.m. on Dec. 7, 2022, in the 10100 block of East Harvard Avenue, where Omara fatally stabbed Taylor Trevino, 20, who died at the scene, and Christina Hardin, 56, who died at the hospital.

Shortly after the stabbings, police responded to a nearby location in the 2500 block of South Dayton Way, where Omara was found being restrained by construction workers after attacking employees there. Officers arrested him and later connected him to the double homicide.

Omara faces life in prison without parole. His sentencing is scheduled for July 24.

stories.headline: Northglenn Woman Killed After Alleged Drunk Driver Crashes Into Home Following Police Pursuit
stories.summary:
An 18-year-old woman died after an alleged drunk driver fled a Northglenn traffic stop and crashed into a house late Thursday night, police said. Officer Angelo Arias, 18, initially stopped when a Northglenn police officer attempted to pull him over near Washington Street and East 112th Avenue after observing erratic driving, but then fled before crashing into a home in the 10400 block of Clarkson Street.

Arias's female passenger, also 18 and not publicly identified, sustained serious injuries in the crash and later died at the hospital. The crash also ruptured a natural gas line, triggering a CodeRed alert and the evacuation of several nearby homes while utility crews shut off the damaged line.

Arias was arrested at the scene and booked into Adams County jail. He faces charges including vehicular homicide, vehicular assault, vehicular eluding, driving under the influence, illegal consumption of alcohol, and failure to display proof of insurance. He remained jailed Wednesday on $50,000 bond.

stories.headline: Polis Vetoes Six More Bills, Setting Personal Record With 12 Rejections This Session
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis vetoed six bills over two days this week, eclipsing his personal record of 12 vetoes set in 2025 during his final year as term-limited governor. Among the most closely watched rejections was Senate Bill 134, which would have prohibited credit card companies from charging swipe fees on sales tax collections starting in 2028. The bill had passed the Senate 18-17 and the House 44-20, but faced fierce opposition from the Electronic Payments Coalition, which spent $6 million on advertising and hired former U.S. Rep. Ed Perlmutter as a lobbyist.

Polis said SB 134 presented too much legal risk to Colorado's business environment with limited upside for small businesses. Other bills vetoed Wednesday included a measure to help firefighters with cancer and one allowing people to sue federal immigration agents for constitutional violations. The Colorado Restaurant Association publicly criticized the governor for vetoing the swipe fee bill, with one Denver restaurant owner saying the decision favored big business over small operators.

In a separate round of vetoes, Polis also rejected bills addressing algorithmic 'surveillance pricing,' arbitration reform, and plastic waste from restaurant takeout. The governor said the surveillance pricing bill was too broad and could penalize legitimate discounting, while the arbitration bill was too vague. Polis encouraged sponsors of both measures to refine and reintroduce the proposals in future legislative sessions.

stories.headline: Historic Denver Asia Center Redevelopment Withdrawn After Community Backlash
stories.summary:
Plans for a $40 million redevelopment of the Asia Center at 1000 S. Federal Blvd. have been officially withdrawn, according to the city's permitting portal and Denver District 7 Councilwoman Flor Alvidrez. The proposal, filed in late April, would have replaced the shopping center with a four-story mixed-use development featuring apartments above ground-floor retail, drawing nearly 19,000 petition signatures in opposition and concerns from tenants who said they were caught off guard.

Alvidrez confirmed that the most recent concept plans were not approved, and that no demolition permits or redevelopment approvals had been granted. She said any future proposal would require additional review, community engagement, and city processes, and that the conversation would likely unfold over several years.

The Asia Center sits along Federal Boulevard in the city-designated Little Saigon Business District and is near planned stops on a new bus rapid transit line. Alvidrez said her office is continuing outreach to small-business tenants and is coordinating with CDOT, city planning, and transportation departments to provide support during the BRT construction process.

stories.headline: Thunderstorms Trigger Ground Stop at Denver International Airport, Causing 480 Delays
stories.summary:
Thunderstorms brought Denver International Airport to a halt Wednesday afternoon, with the FAA ordering a full ground stop at 3:41 p.m. as high winds and hail struck parts of the Front Range. The ground stop was downgraded to a ground delay just before 5 p.m., and the airport was placed on a departure delay at 6:43 p.m., with outbound flights delayed an average of 15 minutes.

As of 7 p.m. Wednesday, airlines at DIA reported 480 delayed flights, led by United Airlines with 163 delays, Southwest with 129, and SkyWest with 122, according to flight-tracking site FlightAware. Forecasters also warned of damaging hail in Douglas and Weld counties during the same storm system.

stories.headline: Toddler Dies After Falling Into Irrigation Ditch in Northern Colorado
stories.summary:
A toddler died after apparently falling into an irrigation ditch in Northern Colorado, according to reports Wednesday, the latest in a string of water-related incidents in the state as summer approaches. Separately, a 4-year-old boy also died following a water rescue in the area on the same day, according to Denver7.

The incidents come as water levels in irrigation ditches and rivers remain elevated in parts of the state. Authorities have not released the identities of the children or the precise locations of both incidents. Officials are urging families to take extra precautions near open water as warm weather increases outdoor activity.

stories.headline: Rising Mortgage Rates Slow Denver Home Sales to Nearly 7% Below Last Year's Pace
stories.summary:
Home and condo sales in the 11-county metro Denver area fell 4.3% in May to 4,004 transactions compared to April, and dropped nearly 7% compared to May 2025, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' monthly report. New listings also declined, falling 9.5% from April and 17.5% from a year earlier, as both buyers and sellers pulled back amid renewed interest rate pressure.

The median price of a single-family home in May was $675,000, up 1.5% from both the prior month and the prior year, while the median condo and townhome price was $395,000, up 2.6% from April but down 2.5% year-over-year. At the current 30-year mortgage rate of 6.5%, the monthly payment on a median-priced home with a 10% down payment runs $3,498, compared to $2,580 at the 3.8% rate seen in May 2020.

DAMAR Market Trends Committee chair Amanda Snitcker described a state of 'homeownership attainability fatigue' on both sides of the market, with inspection contingencies, seller concessions, and rate buydown negotiations becoming more common. She noted that rates rose after the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran in late February, pushing rates back above 6% after briefly dipping below that threshold earlier this year.

stories.headline: Federal Buildings in Denver's Five Points Neighborhood Sought as Local Landmarks to Prevent Demolition
stories.summary:
The General Services Administration is seeking local landmark designation for two historic federal garage buildings in Denver's Five Points neighborhood as the federal government moves to sell off property. The landmark status, if granted, would protect the structures from demolition during or after the sale process.

The GSA has been selling off federal properties across the country as part of a broader government efficiency effort. Local preservationists and city officials are watching the Five Points buildings closely given their historical significance to the neighborhood, one of Denver's oldest and most culturally distinct communities.

Any future redevelopment would be subject to Denver's landmark review process if the designation is approved.

stories.headline: Denver City's New 'Shift 2' Program Offers Gift Card Raffles to Commuters Who Ditch Solo Car Trips
stories.summary:
The Denver Department of Transportation and Infrastructure launched the 'Shift 2 City Wide' program, which offers $1,000 in monthly gift card raffles to residents and workers who swap solo car trips for walking, biking, scooting, carpooling, or public transit at least twice a week. Participants log trips on an online city dashboard that tracks individual metrics including money saved and carbon dioxide emissions reduced.

Monthly participants who meet the two-trips-per-week threshold also receive discounts at local businesses and restaurants in addition to raffle entries. DOTI Executive Director Amy Ford cited current gas prices as an added incentive for residents to explore alternative transportation options.

The program is designed to ease traffic congestion, improve air quality, reduce carbon emissions, and increase physical activity. Participants can also sync their Strava fitness accounts with the city's dashboard.

stories.headline: Chef Theo Adley Opens French-Inspired Heretik Restaurant in Denver's RiNo District
stories.summary:
Chef Theo Adley has opened Heretik, a new restaurant in Denver's River North Art District that draws inspiration from the Côte d'Argent region of southwestern France while incorporating Spanish techniques and a hands-on, convivial dining approach. The menu, which Adley said was written last fall and largely unchanged since, features dishes such as chicken wings stuffed with boudin blanc, Dungeness crab claw cocktail, foie gras, and a seafood plateau ranging from $78 to $150.

Adley previously worked at notable Colorado restaurants including The Flagstaff House in Boulder, The Little Nell in Aspen, and The Squeaky Bean and The Populist in Denver. The wine list is exclusively French and Spanish, and the restaurant's soundtrack features indie rock bands, contributing to what reviewers describe as an intentionally informal yet refined atmosphere.

Heretik joins a growing roster of notable dining openings in RiNo. Adley said the goal was to create a restaurant that balances elegance with approachability, where fine ingredients are paired with tactile, communal eating experiences.

stories.headline: Broncos OTAs Begin With Bo Nix Rehab Timeline, Jaylen Waddle Debut as Key Storylines
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos opened their first of two weeks of organized team activities Tuesday, with reporters set to get their initial look at the team Thursday. The most closely watched storyline is quarterback Bo Nix's recovery from a fractured ankle suffered in January's playoff win over Buffalo and a subsequent second procedure in late April. Head coach Sean Payton has said Nix will likely participate during mandatory minicamp June 16-18 and expects him at full speed before training camp in late July.

Also drawing attention is wide receiver Jaylen Waddle, acquired from Miami in March, who will take the field with his new team for the first time during OTAs. Waddle is expected to form a dynamic pairing with Courtland Sutton at the top of the receiver depth chart, affecting the roles of Troy Franklin, Pat Bryant, and Marvin Mims Jr.

The Broncos' mandatory minicamp is scheduled for June 16-18, with training camp to follow in late July before the team's 'Monday Night Football' regular-season opener Sept. 14 at Kansas City.

stories.headline: Rockies Complete Road Series Win Over Angels, Sweep Within Reach
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies won the first two games of a three-game series at Angel Stadium, setting up a chance for their second road sweep of the 2026 season in Wednesday night's finale. Monday's win came on a Hunter Goodman three-run home run followed by a TJ Rumfield walk-off RBI, while Tuesday's 8-2 victory featured home runs from Goodman, Rumfield, and Willi Castro, who drove in three runs.

Michael Lorenzen was set to start the series finale for Colorado, coming in with a 2-7 record and 7.22 ERA on the season. The Angels countered with Walbert Urena, who entered with a 2-4 record but a strong 2.44 ERA over 44.1 innings pitched.

The Rockies, who have struggled throughout recent seasons, have shown a consistent ability to produce runs on the road in this series, with multiple players contributing across both wins.

joke.joke: My wife woke me up last night and said, 'There's a burglar in the kitchen eating the leftover casserole!' I said, 'Go back to sleep. At least someone's eating it.'

topStory.headline: House Passes Iran War Powers Resolution in First Successful Rebuke of Trump's Undeclared War
topStory.summary:
The U.S. House of Representatives passed a war powers resolution Wednesday directing President Donald Trump to remove American armed forces from hostilities with Iran unless Congress formally declares war or authorizes the use of military force. The measure passed 215 to 208, with four Republicans — Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania, Thomas Massie of Kentucky, Tom Barrett of Michigan and Warren Davidson of Ohio — breaking from their party to side with a unanimous Democratic caucus. It marks the first time this year that such a resolution has cleared the chamber after three previous attempts failed.

The resolution was introduced by Rep. Gregory Meeks of New York, the ranking Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee. Republican leaders, including Speaker Mike Johnson, opposed the measure, arguing it undermines Trump's leverage in ongoing nuclear negotiations with Iran. Foreign Affairs Committee Chairman Brian Mast of Florida dismissed the vote as a purely political gesture, saying it weakens the president's negotiating position. A White House official noted that several Republican members were absent for the vote and pointed to the failure of earlier similar resolutions.

The vote had been expected before the Memorial Day recess on May 21 but was abruptly pulled from the floor when it appeared too many Republicans were absent to block it. Johnson adjourned the chamber early in a move widely interpreted as an effort to prevent the resolution from passing. It was revived after the recess and succeeded Wednesday. Rep. Barrett, an Army veteran whose Michigan seat is considered vulnerable in November's midterm elections, said Trump's authority under the War Powers Act of 1973 had expired and that Congress must now decide the scope and limits of the mission.

The United States joined Israel in attacking Iran on February 28, a conflict that will reach its 100th day Saturday. Trump has sought to characterize the military engagement as a "skirmish" or "short-term excursion" and did not seek congressional authorization before committing forces. The Constitution grants Congress the sole authority to declare war, a provision that has been a point of contention throughout the conflict.

The House resolution is largely symbolic and would not compel Trump to end the war; he could also veto any binding legislation that reaches his desk. However, the vote provides momentum for a Senate version of the resolution that does carry binding language requiring Trump to end the war without congressional approval. That measure has already advanced on the Senate floor but has not received a final vote. For it to become law, it would need to pass the House and survive a presidential veto.
